subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Component counts visible in the provided rulebook pages are:

Psychic-side components:
- 6 intuition tokens (1 of each color)
- 6 sleeves (1 of each color)
- 6 clairvoyancy level markers (1 of each color)
- 36 clairvoyancy tokens (6 of each color)
- 1 clock board
- 4 progress boards total: 1 character progress board, 1 location progress board, 1 object progress board, 1 epilogue progress board
- 54 psychic cards total, split into 18 character psychic cards (rear numbered 1-18), 18 location psychic cards (rear numbered 19-36), and 18 object psychic cards (rear numbered 37-54)

Ghost-side components:
- 1 game screen
- 1 sand timer
- 54 ghost cards total, split into 18 character ghost cards (rear numbered 1-18), 18 location ghost cards (rear numbered 19-36), and 18 object ghost cards (rear numbered 37-54)
- 84 vision cards
- 6 ghost tokens (front: 1 of each color)
- 6 culprit tokens (rear numbered 1-6)
- 1 clairvoyancy track board

Gameplay landmarks printed on boards:
- Clock board has a clock and a clairvoyancy-token discard area.
- Epilogue board has a culprit space and a mission accomplished space.

Player-distribution counts from setup text:
- In 2-3 player games: each psychic receives 0 clairvoyancy voting tokens.
- In 4-5 player games: each psychic receives 4 clairvoyancy tokens (numbered 1-4).
- In 6-7 player games: each psychic receives 6 clairvoyancy tokens (numbered 1-6).

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
Set up in this order so the table state is easy to read:

1) Place the clock board where everyone can see it; set hour hand to I (1h).
2) Place the progress boards in sequence for easy advancement: character, location, object, then epilogue.
3) Place the clairvoyancy track board where all players can move markers (used in 4-7 player games).
4) Lay out psychic card displays (character/location/object) according to chosen difficulty and player count as instructed by setup tables.
5) Seat the ghost behind the game screen with hidden info only on their side.
6) Behind the screen, arrange the selected ghost cards into columns by psychic color and rows by card type (character row, location row, object row), and place matching ghost tokens at the bottom of each column as reminders.
7) Put the vision card deck behind the screen; ghost draws 7 vision cards as starting hand.
8) Keep the sand timer reachable for the ghost to start after all visions are distributed each turn.
9) Place culprit tokens with ghost until final phase.

Practical table tip: keep solved cards in each psychic’s sleeve immediately as they are confirmed. This prevents accidental re-checking of already solved clues and keeps progression visible to all players.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Choose roles first: exactly one player is the ghost; all others are psychics.

Each psychic takes:
- 1 intuition token (matching chosen color)
- 1 sleeve (matching color)
- 1 clairvoyancy level marker set to 0 on clairvoyancy track
- Clairvoyancy voting tokens based on player count (0 in 2-3p, 4 tokens numbered 1-4 in 4-5p, or 6 tokens numbered 1-6 in 6-7p)

The ghost takes:
- Game screen
- Ghost cards and vision cards
- Ghost tokens and culprit tokens
- Sand timer

Difficulty setup (easy/medium/hard) determines:
- How many character/location/object psychic cards are laid out
- How many times the ghost may discard and redraw vision cards using crow/discard allowances

Assignment of hidden solutions:
For each psychic, the ghost creates one hidden combination of exactly three cards: one character (suspect), one location, one object. Those combinations are placed behind the screen in columns corresponding to psychic colors.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
Game starts in Phase 1: Reconstruction of events. Time limit is seven turns (hours I through VII).

Initial state before first turn:
- Every psychic places their intuition token on the character progress board.
- Ghost has 7 vision cards in hand.
- Ghost knows each psychic’s assigned character/location/object combination.

Shared objective and order of investigation:
Each psychic must solve in strict order:
1) their assigned character,
2) then their assigned location,
3) then their assigned object.

Only after all psychics finish all three before time expires does the game enter Phase 2 (Revealing the culprit). If even one psychic is still unresolved when the seventh hour ends, the game is immediately lost.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each game turn in Phase 1 follows these numbered steps:

1. Vision Interpretation step begins
   - Ghost gives one or more vision cards to each psychic (face up in front of that psychic), refilling hand to 7 between deliveries.
   - Ghost marks serviced psychics with their color ghost token against the screen.

2. Psychics discuss and choose
   - Each psychic interprets their current vision pile and places intuition token onto one candidate card in the active board row (character, location, or object depending on their progress).
   - In 4-7 player games, psychics may place agree/disagree clairvoyancy tokens on other players’ intuition choices before timer expires.

3. Sand timer ends discussion window
   - Once timer runs out, intuition placements are evaluated by ghost.

4. Spectral Manifestation
   - Ghost confirms each psychic as correct or incorrect in any order.
   - Resolve progression, card movement, and clairvoyancy gains accordingly.

5. Advance hour hand
   - Move clock forward one hour.

6. Check transition condition
   - If all psychics solved all three stages in time, move to Phase 2.
   - If not all solved and clock has reached/passed VII end condition, all players lose.
   - Otherwise, begin next turn of Phase 1.

Phase 2 (if reached) has three steps:
1) Suspect line-up,
2) Shared vision,
3) Straw poll and reveal.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Ghost actions each Phase-1 turn:
- Select one psychic, place one or more vision cards in front of them.
- Push that psychic’s color ghost token against screen to mark they received cards this turn.
- Draw back up to 7 vision cards after each delivery.
- Repeat until every psychic has at least one new card this turn.

Ghost discard/redraw option (difficulty dependent):
- The ghost may discard any/all current vision cards and redraw to 7, but only up to the allowed number of uses at chosen difficulty.
- EASY: once per turn.
- MEDIUM: up to three times per game.
- HARD: once per game.

Psychic actions each Phase-1 turn:
- Review all vision cards currently in front of you (including retained cards from prior misses).
- Discuss openly with table.
- Place intuition token on one target candidate card in your current category row.
- Move/change your intuition token any time before timer ends.

Clairvoyancy voting actions (4-7 players):
- You may place agree/disagree tokens on other psychics’ intuition tokens (never on your own).
- You may place multiple clairvoyancy tokens in one turn, but only one token per target intuition token.
- Tokens can be moved/removed before timer expires.
- After resolution, used clairvoyancy tokens go to discard area and are retrieved as specified (start of fourth turn).

Resolution outcomes:
If a psychic guessed correctly:
- Ghost flips corresponding hidden ghost card in that psychic’s column.
- Any players who correctly predicted that psychic’s outcome with clairvoyancy tokens advance their clairvoyancy marker by one.
- Psychic takes solved card, places it in sleeve, discards their vision cards, and advances intuition token to next progress board.

If a psychic guessed incorrectly:
- Correct clairvoyancy predictors still advance marker by one.
- Psychic stays on same progress board category.
- Psychic keeps accumulated vision cards; ghost adds more on future turns until solved or game ends.

When a psychic completes all three categories:
- Move their intuition token to mission accomplished on epilogue board.
- Advance their clairvoyancy marker one space per hour remaining on clock.
- They continue helping discussion and may continue playing remaining clairvoyancy tokens in support of others.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Asymmetric communication model:
Mysterium is intentionally one-way and image-based. Ghost has full hidden assignment info but cannot speak clues; psychics must infer from abstract image associations. The rulebook explicitly warns that ghost should never use comments or gestures that function as clues.

Progress-board order lock:
Psychics must solve character first, then location, then object. You cannot skip ahead even if a later clue appears obvious.

Clairvoyancy track impact:
In 4-7 player games, your clairvoyancy position determines how much information you receive during final culprit vote:
- Low level sees fewer shared-vision cards before voting.
- Intermediate sees more.
- High sees all three shared-vision cards.
This creates a second objective during Phase 1: not just solving your own line, but accurately reading teammates.

Phase 2 structure details:
Step 1 - Suspect line-up:
- Remove unused psychic cards and progress boards from table.
- Make grouped sets (character+location+object) from solved sleeves and label groups with numbered ghost tokens.

Step 2 - Shared vision:
- Ghost chooses 3 vision cards that all indicate one target group (one card conceptually pointing to character, one to location, one to object), shuffles and places face down.
- Ghost secretly sets matching culprit token face down on culprit space.

Step 3 - Straw poll:
- Psychics vote secretly (2-3p variant differs) for which group is culprit.
- Voting timing depends on clairvoyancy bracket.
- Majority group becomes team’s official accusation; ties are broken by highest clairvoyancy position, then eldest player if needed.

2-3 player adjustments:
- Clairvoyancy track and clairvoyancy tokens are not used.
- Each player controls two psychics.
- In shared vision, cards are face up.
- Additional random card groups are added to bring line-up to four groups before final vote.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
Mysterium has no victory points; it is a cooperative win/loss outcome.

How the game ends:

Loss End #1 (time failure):
- During Phase 1, if clock reaches end of seventh hour and at least one psychic has not solved their full character+location+object combination, all players lose immediately.

Proceed condition:
- If all psychics complete their combinations before time runs out, Phase 2 begins.

Final resolution in Phase 2:
1) Psychics organize solved combinations into numbered suspect groups.
2) Ghost provides shared vision and secretly marks true culprit group with culprit token.
3) Psychics conduct straw poll and choose one group as final accusation, using majority and tie-break hierarchy defined in rules.
4) Reveal culprit token.

Win condition:
- If chosen group matches culprit token group, all players win together and ghost is laid to rest.

Loss End #2 (wrong accusation):
- If chosen group does not match culprit token group, all players lose.

Tiebreak logic inside final vote (not score-based):
- First tie-break: group selected by psychic furthest on clairvoyancy track.
- Second tie-break: if still tied, group selected by eldest tied player.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Build a shared interpretation language in turn 1.
In your first discussion, explicitly call out what dimensions your table will prioritize in vision cards: color palette, object motifs, mood, era, motion, symbolism. Teams that align this framework early waste fewer hours on mixed logic.

2) Ghost should front-load clarity, not cleverness.
In early turns, give strong, narrow visions that separate a target from nearby candidates. Overly poetic multi-meaning sets early can stall one psychic for several rounds and burn clock.

3) Psychics should narrow candidates before debating details.
Instead of arguing card-by-card across the whole row, first eliminate obvious non-matches quickly, then compare top two options deeply. This preserves timer bandwidth.

4) In 4-7 player games, spend clairvoyancy tokens proactively.
You need clairvoyancy progress before final phase to see more shared-vision cards. Waiting too long often leaves you low-information at the most important vote.

5) Track table bottlenecks each hour.
If one psychic is behind while others are solved, shift group attention there immediately. Finishing everyone before VII matters more than optimizing already-solved lines.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Treating every image literally.
Many useful clues are thematic, emotional, or compositional rather than object-exact. Literal-only reading misses ghost intent and slows progression.

2) Ghost giving contradictory card bundles.
A multi-card vision should reinforce one candidate, not split across several. If teammates debate two separate interpretations equally, the clue set was probably too broad.

3) Ignoring progression order.
New players sometimes discuss location/object while still unresolved on character. That can be fun chatter but wastes critical time under 7-hour limit.

4) Hoarding clairvoyancy tokens.
In applicable player counts, unused tokens are lost opportunity. Repeated small accurate reads are better than waiting for perfect certainty.

5) Letting solved players disengage.
Once solved, a player still contributes huge value as external analyst. If solved players 'check out,' team deduction quality drops exactly when remaining puzzles are hardest.

6) Overconfidence in final straw poll.
Final phase still requires independent judgment; groupthink can produce tie-break losses. Use your clairvoyancy-informed information tier carefully before revealing votes.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Decision 1: Precision vs flexibility in ghost clueing
A precise clue can solve instantly but fails hard if misread. A broader clue is safer but slower. Ghost should adjust based on clock pressure and each psychic’s current confidence.

Decision 2: Immediate lock-in vs timer-length exploration
Psychics must decide when to commit token placement. Early commitment gives room for support via clairvoyancy and teammate review; late commitment may incorporate more discussion but risks rushed final pivots at timer end.

Decision 3: Clairvoyancy allocation strategy
Should you bet on obvious picks for reliable track gain, or on uncertain high-impact calls where other players are split? Conservative betting improves average gain; selective risk can leapfrog your information tier before final vote.

Decision 4: Assist weakest lane vs optimize strongest lane
When table attention is limited, helping the most blocked psychic is usually correct because Phase 1 fails if anyone is incomplete. However, sometimes ensuring one more psychic reaches mission accomplished sooner can generate additional clairvoyancy position benefits. Teams should reassess this trade-off each hour based on remaining time.

metadata.notes: Written from provided Mysterium rulebook text excerpts as primary source.
